name: Reserve Bank of India
summary: ESCALATING CRISIS RESPONSE: Imposed emergency NOP-INR $100M hard cap on March 28 — first regulatory intervention beyond conventional dollar selling. Forex reserves dropped to $698.35B (below $700B, down $30B in 3 weeks from ATH $728.49B). Net short dollar position approaching $100B. Rupee breached 95 despite intervention. Trapped between inflation (oil $112→can't cut rates) and growth (needs to cut). Repo rate held at 5.25%.

signals.content: RBI created intervention space by changing maturity profile of short dollar forwards, reducing near-term delivery obligations to take on fresh short positions.
RBI sold $18-20B in single week to cushion rupee. Revived heavy pre-market intervention through state-run banks.
RBI announced ₹1 trillion ($10.9B) bond purchases from open market, adding to screen-based purchases. Supporting both currency and bond markets simultaneously.

RBI net short dollar position approaching $100B — effectively betting crisis resolves before reserves run out. Intervening mainly in forward segment rather than spot. Forex reserves dropped $7B to $709.75B. Banking liquidity turned deficit. Repo rate held at 5.25% despite inflation pressure.

RBI imposed emergency NOP-INR hard cap of $100 million on ALL authorized dealer banks regardless of balance sheet size (March 28), compliance deadline April 10. Previously banks could maintain NOP limits up to 25% of capital. This is explicitly anti-speculation and constitutes a form of capital flow management — not full capital controls but significant tightening of forex market architecture. Escalation ladder: conventional intervention (selling dollars) → regulatory caps (NOP limit, this step) → full capital controls (next if NOP cap doesn't hold). Forex reserves dropped to $698.35 billion (below $700B, down $30.14B in 3 weeks of March from ATH $728.49B pre-war). Gold component dropped $13.5B in one week. Net short dollar forward position approaching $100 billion. (Sources: Business Standard, RBI circular, Bloomberg)

edges.context: Central bank. Rate decisions, currency intervention, and bond market support directly affect India's economic trajectory during crisis.
Actively defending rupee. Sold $15B+ in March. Strategy is smooth depreciation, not reversal. Forward reserves being restructured.
If RBI can't cut rates (due to inflation), no domestic growth boost. Markets need rate cuts for valuation support.
Oil above $100 = inflation = RBI can't cut. Oil is the binding constraint on RBI's ability to support growth.

trigger_points.mechanism: Would signal panic mode. Would crash equities but might stabilize rupee temporarily. Last resort.
